prompt: Put the permutation test for the punting slope in order.
steps: Fit the line and record the observed slope b1 = 0.902
Shuffle the 13 distances against the fixed strengths
Refit the shuffled data and store its slope
Repeat the shuffle B = 5000 times to build the null
why: A permutation test builds the no-association null from your own data: you keep the observed slope, then shuffle the response against the fixed predictor many times to see what slopes pure chance produces, and finally place the observed slope in that null with p = (M+1)/(B+1). The shuffle must come before the count, because the count needs a null to compare against. See section 5.2.

prompt: In 5000 reshuffles of the punting data, only 4 gave a slope as steep as the observed 0.902. What is the permutation p-value?
why: The permutation p-value adds one to both the count and the total, giving (M+1)/(B+1) = (4+1)/(5000+1) which is about 0.001. The plus one counts the observed arrangement itself as a valid arrangement under the null, so the p-value can never be exactly zero. Using plain M/B here would understate the smallest honest value. See section 5.2.

prompt: One sentence about the n = 13 punting sample is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The classical t test for the slope is exact only when the errors are normal.
A quantile plot of 13 residuals with a slight bend proves the errors are non-normal.
With only 13 punters there is no large-sample rescue for the t test.
The permutation test needs no normality assumption at all.

why: At n = 13 even genuinely normal data makes a bent, wandering quantile plot, so a slight bend is no proof of non-normality: 13 points cannot tell normal from non-normal. That is exactly why the chapter turns to resampling, which needs no such check. The other three sentences are all correct. See section 5.1.

prompt: The 13 real punters: x is right-leg strength in pounds above 150, y is punt distance in feet. Drag the sliders to fit the least-squares line and get the SSE meter into the green.
why: Least squares picks the slope and intercept that minimize the sum of squared vertical gaps (SSE). For the real punting data the best fit has slope about 0.902 feet per pound and, in these centered units, intercept near 150. That observed slope 0.902 is the number the whole chapter tests and resamples. See section 5.2.

prompt: Match each resampling method to what it does.

pairs.meaning: shuffle Y vs fixed X to get a p-value
resample with replacement to get a standard error
redraw whole rows, so the X values vary
keep the line, reattach reshuffled residuals

why: A permutation test breaks the X to Y link by shuffling and answers whether an effect exists. A bootstrap resamples with replacement to measure how precise the estimate is. Case resampling redraws whole rows so the set of strengths changes; residual resampling holds the design fixed and only redraws errors. See sections 5.2 and 5.4.

prompt: The bootstrap slopes give a percentile interval (0.413, 1.382) and the observed slope is b1 = 0.902. What is the basic (reflection) interval?
options: (0.413, 1.382), the same as the percentile interval
why: The basic interval reflects the percentiles through twice the observed slope: (2b1 - q_0.975, 2b1 - q_0.025) with 2b1 = 1.804 gives (0.422, 1.391). Reflection swaps the tails, so the upper percentile 1.382 becomes the lower endpoint. Here it nearly matches the percentile interval because the bootstrap distribution is close to symmetric. See section 5.5.

prompt: On Toluca, no reshuffle out of B = 5000 reached the observed slope 3.5702, so M = 0. What p-value do you report, and why can it not be smaller?
options: p = 0, because no reshuffle beat the slope
p = (0+1)/(5000+1) = 1/5001, the resolution floor set by B
p = 4.45e-10, matching the t test exactly
p = 1/13, one over the sample size
why: With M = 0 the permutation p-value is (0+1)/(5000+1) = 1/5001, about 0.0002, and never exactly zero. A Monte Carlo permutation test has a floor of 1/(B+1): it can only say the p-value is smaller than it can measure. The t test, if you trust its assumptions, can put a number like 4.45e-10 on the far tail. See section 5.3.
